public int hardestWorker(int n, int[][] logs) {

    int maxDuration = 0;

    int resultId = -1;

    int previousTime = 0;

    for (int[] log : logs) {

        int currentId = log[0];

        int currentLeave = log[1];

        int duration = currentLeave - previousTime;

        if (duration > maxDuration) {

            maxDuration = duration;

            resultId = currentId;

        } else if (duration == maxDuration) {

            if (currentId < resultId) {

                resultId = currentId;

            }

        } else {

            // do nothing

        }

        previousTime = currentLeave;

    }

    return resultId;

}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int hardestWorker(int n, int[][] logs) {
        int maxDuration = 0;
        int resultId = -1;
        int previousTime = 0;
        for (int[] log : logs) {
            int currentId = log[0];
            int currentLeave = log[1];
            int duration = currentLeave - previousTime;
            if (duration > maxDuration) {
                maxDuration = duration;
                resultId = currentId;
            } else if (duration == maxDuration) {
                if (currentId < resultId) {
                    resultId = currentId;
                }
            }
            previousTime = currentLeave;
        }
        return resultId;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(m) where m is the number of logs
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution calculates each task's duration by subtracting the previous task's end time from the current task's end time. It tracks the maximum duration and selects the smallest employee ID in case of ties. The approach is efficient with linear time complexity and constant space, but requires the logs to be sorted by leaveTime as per the problem constraints.
